planting.note: Start indoors about six weeks before the last frost, then move out once nights stay mild.

directions: Press seed onto warm, moist mix and leave it uncovered, since a little light speeds sprouting. Harden off seedlings before setting them into lean, fast-draining soil in a hot, sunny spot. Expect semi-double tangerine-orange blooms with chocolate centers from early summer onward; the plant needs no deadheading to keep flowering.

planting.note: Sow indoors six weeks ahead of the last frost and transplant into warm soil.

directions: Surface-sow onto warm soil and mist lightly, keeping the seed uncovered until germination. Set out compact, mounding plants in full sun and dry, gritty soil where they will spill nicely over container edges. Bright golden-yellow daisies with dark eyes cover the low mats from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost danger passes and soil is thoroughly warm.

directions: Scatter seed over prepared ground and press in gently without burying, as light helps it start. This semi-trailing selection wants full sun and sharp drainage and shrugs off heat and drought once established. Golden semi-double flowers with dark centers bloom nonstop over spreading stems until the first frost.

planting.note: Begin indoors about six weeks before the last frost for earlier bloom.

directions: Leave the fine seed uncovered on warm, damp mix and keep it bright until seedlings emerge. Transplant into full sun and lean, well-drained soil, spacing plants so their mats can knit together. Deep golden daisies with near-black centers flower profusely from early summer to frost with no deadheading needed.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm, well-drained soil after the last frost.

directions: Sow on the surface and firm lightly, since burying the seed too deep will prevent it from sprouting. This low, carpeting selection thrives in poor, dry soils and full sun, making a tidy edging or groundcover. Small single yellow daisies with dark eyes blanket the mat all summer into autumn.

planting.note: Start seed indoors six weeks before setting out after frost.

directions: Press the seed onto warm mix and keep it uncovered and evenly moist until it germinates. Grow these compact, free-flowering plants in full sun and gritty, dry soil, ideal for pots and window boxes. Cheerful golden flowers with dark centers appear early and keep coming right up to frost.

planting.note: Sow indoors around six weeks before the last frost for container use.

directions: Leave seed uncovered on warm, moist media and provide bright light to trigger germination. This trailing series member fills baskets and containers in full sun with little water once rooted. Bright yellow daisies with dark button centers cascade over the pot edge from summer to frost.

planting.note: Start indoors six weeks ahead of the last frost and move out when warm.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it exposed to light on warm, damp mix until it sprouts. Set the trailing plants into full sun and free-draining soil where they can spill from baskets and beds. Warm orange daisies with dark eyes flower generously all season without any deadheading.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Plant nursery-grown starts of this vegetative variety at the same depth they grew in the pot. Give them full sun, good drainage, and light feeding for strong trailing growth in baskets and mixed containers. Golden-yellow daisies with dark centers bloom continuously and self-clean from late spring until frost.

planting.note: Transplant rooted cuttings outdoors once nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Set purchased plants into rich, well-drained potting mix or garden soil in full sun. Feed regularly and water when the top inch dries, as vigorous trailing growth appreciates steadier moisture than seed strains. Masses of small golden flowers with dark eyes trail over edges from spring until the first hard frost.

planting.note: Move rooted plants outdoors after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant rooted starts at their original depth in full sun and free-draining soil or mix. Keep them evenly moist and lightly fed to fuel the spreading, cascading habit in baskets and pots. Warm orange daisies with dark centers flower without pause and need no deadheading through the season.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after frost, spacing for trailing containers.

directions: Transplant nursery starts of this cutting-grown variety into sunny, well-drained containers or beds. Water moderately and feed lightly to sustain steady, spreading growth through hot weather. Golden-yellow daisies with dark button centers cover the trailing stems from spring until frost.

planting.note: Sow indoors in early spring and set out hardened seedlings after frost danger passes.

directions: Barely cover seed and germinate at room temperature, then transplant to a sunny bed with light, freely draining soil. This deep maroon selection blooms best with regular deadheading and appreciates a support net where stems reach knee height. Snip flowers for arrangements when the buds are just cracking open for the longest vase life.

planting.note: Start seed under cover six to eight weeks ahead and plant out after the last frost.

directions: Press seed lightly into a fine seed mix and keep it warm and moist until it germinates. Prized for its almost-black, honey-scented flowers, this tall selection wants full sun and benefits from staking as it lengthens. Harvest stems when the outer florets begin to unfurl, and keep cutting or deadheading to prolong the display.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors and move them out once nights stay above freezing.

directions: Sow shallowly in trays, thin to the strongest seedlings, and plant into a sunny, well-drained spot. The soft peach-to-cream blooms deepen in tone as they age and draw in bees and butterflies. Cut freely for bouquets and remove faded heads to encourage a steady succession through summer.

planting.note: Begin under glass in spring and transplant after hardening off past the frost date.

directions: Cover seed lightly and keep it moist and warm for even germination before planting out. This wine-red selection is a reliable cut flower that blooms over a long season with routine deadheading. Pick stems in the cool of the morning as the buds start to open, and support tall plants in exposed gardens.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and set plants out when the soil has warmed after frost.

directions: Start seed shallowly in trays and grow on in bright light before transplanting to full sun. The warm salmon-pink flowers are excellent for cutting and stand up well in the vase. Keep the plants deadheaded or harvested regularly, cutting stems just as the florets begin to open.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and plant out after the danger of frost has passed.

directions: Sow seed thinly under cover, barely covering it, and keep the surface evenly moist until sprouting. This pure-white form lights up a border and mixes beautifully in bouquets with darker shades. Deadhead spent flowers and cut stems young to keep the plant blooming into early autumn.

planting.note: Start seed in spring under cover and transplant after the last spring frost.

directions: Cover seed lightly and germinate warm, then grow seedlings on before planting into free-draining soil in full sun. The lavender-blue pincushions are magnets for pollinators and last well when cut. Pick or deadhead often, taking stems as the outer florets first open for the longest-lasting blooms.

planting.note: Sow indoors six to eight weeks before setting plants out after frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it moist until germination, then transplant to a sunny, sheltered bed. This tall, large-flowered lavender-blue selection makes a superb long-stemmed cut flower. Stake in windy sites, deadhead regularly, and cut stems just as the buds break for the vase.

planting.note: Start seed under cover and move hardened seedlings out once frost has passed.

directions: Sow shallowly in trays and grow on in good light before planting to full sun and well-drained soil. The rich crimson-scarlet flowers are sweetly scented and draw in bees all summer. Keep the plants cut or deadheaded, harvesting stems as the first florets open to extend flowering.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors and plant them out after the final spring frost.

directions: Press seed into fine mix, cover lightly, and keep warm until it germinates. This deep blackish-maroon form has protruding pale stamens that give it a starry look and it thrives in heat. Deadhead or cut regularly, taking stems as the buds start to open for arrangements.

planting.note: Sow indoors in early spring and transplant after frost for cut-flower rows.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and grow strong transplants for setting into full sun once frost risk ends. Bred for the cut-flower trade, this blend gives long, sturdy stems across a mix of white, pink, red and deep purple shades. Harvest when the outer florets just open and keep cutting to drive continuous production.

planting.note: Start under cover and plant out after frost for long cutting stems.

directions: Barely cover seed, germinate warm, and grow on before transplanting to a sunny, well-drained bed. This cut-flower selection delivers uniform, long-stemmed scarlet-red pincushions ideal for bouquets. Cut stems as the buds crack open and keep harvesting to encourage fresh flowering.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and set out after the last frost for cutting.

directions: Sow seed thinly and lightly covered, then grow vigorous seedlings for planting into full sun. Selected for cut-flower uniformity, it produces long stems topped with deep burgundy-red blooms. Harvest as the first ring of florets opens and cut often to keep new buds forming.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring and plant out once frost danger has passed.

directions: Sow shallowly under cover and transplant strong seedlings to a sunny, free-draining bed. This blend gathers berry tones from deep plum and crimson through raspberry and soft pink for a rich cutting patch. Deadhead and cut regularly, taking stems just as the buds begin to open.

planting.note: Start indoors early and set out after frost for tall, large-flowered plants.

directions: Cover seed lightly, germinate warm, and grow on before planting to a sunny bed with room to branch. This tall strain offers large double blooms in a wide mix of colors on long, cut-worthy stems. Stake where exposed, deadhead spent heads, and cut stems as the buds open for the vase.

planting.note: Direct sow after the soil warms or start indoors for earlier bloom.

directions: Sow seed about an eighth of an inch deep and thin seedlings to give each plant room. This compact, bushy mix suits the front of a border, containers, and edging with a range of double flowers. Deadhead often to keep it tidy and blooming, and cut a few short stems for small posies.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and plant out after frost for tall cutting stems.

directions: Cover seed lightly and germinate warm, then transplant to full sun and well-drained ground. This classic tall mix produces fully double, sweetly scented flowers in many colors on long stems for cutting. Support in windy spots, deadhead or harvest often, and cut stems as the buds start to open.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and set plants out after the last hard frost.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and cover the tray to exclude light, since darkness aids germination at cool room temperatures. Pinch young plants once to encourage a bushy, well-branched shape and keep them evenly moist but never soggy. Expect a long flush of orchid-like blooms from late spring once cool weather holds; feed lightly every couple of weeks during flowering.

planting.note: Raise seedlings under cover and transplant once frost has passed.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist mix and keep it dark until sprouts appear, thinning to the strongest plants. Provide light, free-draining soil and shelter from strong wind, which can snap the ferny stems. The species carries loose sprays of streaked, butterfly-like flowers through the cooler months of the growing season.

planting.note: Sow indoors in early spring; move out when nights stay above freezing.

directions: Barely cover the seed and germinate in darkness at moderate warmth. This Chilean species grows taller and more open than the garden hybrids, so give it a little support or a sheltered spot. It blooms in soft pink to lilac tones with a yellow throat once the plants are established in cool conditions.

planting.note: Start under glass in late winter and harden off before planting out.

directions: Surface-sow onto a fine, moist medium and keep the container dark until seedlings emerge. Grow on cool and bright to avoid soft, drawn stems, and pinch once for fuller growth. This species offers finely marked flowers in rose and violet shades over ferny foliage during the cool season.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors and transplant after frost danger ends.

directions: Sow the small seed shallowly, cover for darkness, and keep temperatures moderate for even sprouting. Site in fertile, well-drained soil with shelter from midday heat, which shortens the bloom period. The plant produces clusters of predominantly white, yellow-centered flowers on branching stems.

planting.note: Begin indoors in early spring and set out once the soil has warmed.

directions: Germinate the seed in darkness at cool room warmth and grow the seedlings on in bright, airy conditions. This taller Chilean species benefits from a little staking and steady moisture through its flowering stretch. It carries showy rose-pink flowers marked with an orange-yellow throat.

planting.note: Sow under cover in late winter and plant out after the last frost.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on moist mix, exclude light, and keep the temperature moderate until germination. Grow cool and give free-draining soil, as this species naturally hails from coastal Chilean slopes. Expect bright pink to magenta flowers over lacy foliage during the cooler part of the season.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant after frost for early-summer color.

directions: Sow shallowly and cover the tray to darken it until seedlings appear at cool room temperature. This compact, well-branched mix needs little pinching and suits pots, borders, and window boxes. It delivers a dense show of orchid-like flowers in a wide blend of pinks, roses, and lilacs.

planting.note: Raise seedlings early indoors and plant out once frost has passed.

directions: Press seed onto damp mix, keep it dark, and grow the seedlings cool and bright to stay stocky. This dwarf mix stays neat without staking and makes a strong container or edging plant. It blooms heavily in a bright range of pink, red, and violet shades with contrasting throats.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for tall plants to set out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow, exclude light for germination, and grow on cool to build sturdy stems. This classic tall mix appreciates light staking and shelter from wind in an open border. It produces large, richly marked flowers across a full spectrum of colors over a long season.

planting.note: Sow early under cover; transplant once nights stay frost-free.

directions: Sow the fine seed shallowly and keep the tray dark and evenly moist until sprouts show. This very compact, mounding selection needs no pinching and is ideal for small pots and the front of a bed. It smothers itself in small orchid-like flowers in mixed colors during cool weather.

planting.note: Start indoors and move out after the last frost for containers or beds.

directions: Press seed onto moist mix, cover to darken, and germinate at cool room temperature. This tidy, early mix branches freely and holds up well in pots and mixed plantings. It flowers profusely in a soft blend of pastel and jewel tones with delicate central markings.

planting.note: Set out hardened seedlings around the last spring frost for silver bedding.

directions: Start seed indoors on the surface under bright light six to eight weeks before your last frost, as it needs light to germinate. Grow in lean, well-drained soil and water only when the top inch dries, since the felted foliage rots in wet ground. Pinch off any yellow summer flower stalks to keep the compact, lacy silver mounds looking their best.

planting.note: Transplant near the last frost for broad, oak-like silver leaves.

directions: Surface-sow indoors under light about eight weeks before transplanting out, keeping the mix barely moist until sprouts appear. This selection has broader, less-divided silvery leaves than the lacy types, so give each plant a little more room. Keep it on the dry side and remove flower buds to preserve the bold white foliage.

planting.note: Plant out around last frost for rounded, felted silver leaves.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a sterile mix indoors and keep it warm and lightly moist until germination, then grow on in bright light. The rounded, minimally lobed leaves have a thick white felt that resents overhead watering, so water at the base. Trim back leggy growth in midsummer to encourage dense, mounded silver clumps.

planting.note: Set transplants out near last frost for a uniform silver edging.

directions: Sow indoors on the soil surface under lights six to eight weeks ahead of planting time, as darkness suppresses germination. This vigorous, uniform strain holds its shape well in heat and needs only lean soil and sparing water. Snip out flower stems as they form to keep the neat silver habit for edging and containers.

planting.note: Transplant around last frost for tall, brightly whitened foliage.

directions: Start seed indoors on the surface under bright light, keeping temperatures steady until seedlings emerge. This taller selection carries especially white, deeply cut leaves and makes a strong contrast plant toward the middle of a bed. Grow hard in poor, dry soil and remove bloom stalks to keep the foliage crisp and pale.

planting.note: Plant out near last frost; overwinters in mild-winter regions.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface indoors, as it needs light to sprout, then grow on in a bright, cool spot. In frost-free zones the plant persists as a woody-based subshrub and produces small yellow daisy flowers in its second summer. Cut it back hard in spring to renew fresh silver growth if you carry it over.

planting.note: Grow as a cool-season pot plant; time sowings for spring color.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and keep it at a steady mild temperature with bright, indirect light until germination. Grow the plants cool, around fifty to sixty degrees, and never let the pot dry fully or the leaves wilt sharply. Expect dense domes of daisy flowers in blue, purple, pink, and red once the plants have bulked up, usually four to six months from sowing.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool spring soil where plants are to flower.

directions: Scatter seed thinly over well-drained soil and barely cover it, thinning seedlings once they are a few inches tall. This South African annual likes cool weather and sandy ground and can flop in rich soil, so hold back on feeding. It carries loose clusters of magenta-purple daisies with yellow centers from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Plant nursery starts after frost for broad silver leaves.

directions: Set out rooted plants in gritty, sharply drained soil once nights stay above freezing, spacing them for good airflow. The oversized, felted silver leaves scorch in harsh afternoon sun in hot climates and rot in soggy ground, so give bright light with lean, dry conditions. Remove any tattered leaves through the season and overwinter it indoors where frosts are hard.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring; grow as a houseplant in most regions.

directions: Lay a cutting on top of gritty cactus mix so the bead-like leaves touch the soil, and it will root along the stem within a few weeks. Give bright, indirect light and water only when the mix is fully dry, as the trailing strands rot quickly if kept wet. Mature plants dangle in long ropes and produce small white brush-like flowers with a cinnamon scent.

planting.note: Start from cuttings in spring; usually grown indoors or in frost-free gardens.

directions: Press stem cuttings into fast-draining succulent mix and keep them barely moist until roots form, which happens readily. It is faster and more forgiving than string of pearls, tolerating brighter light and drying fully between waterings. The banana-shaped leaves trail vigorously and the plant occasionally opens small white flowers with a spicy fragrance.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring; keep as a houseplant outside frost-free zones.

directions: Set cuttings on gritty mix and let the plump, tear-shaped leaves make contact so they root along the stem. Provide bright, indirect light and water only when thoroughly dry, since standing moisture causes rapid rot. The oval beads often show faint translucent lines and the trailing stems suit hanging pots.

planting.note: Plant divisions or cuttings in spring in gritty, well-drained ground.

directions: Push cuttings directly into lean, sharply drained soil where they root with little fuss. This low, spreading species forms a mat of powdery blue-green finger leaves and is happiest in full sun with minimal water. Shear it lightly to keep it dense, and expect small off-white summer flowers that many gardeners simply trim away.

planting.note: Set cuttings in spring in full sun and fast-draining soil.

directions: Break off pieces and plant them into gritty soil, where they root quickly and knit into a blue carpet. The upright, pencil-like leaves are a vivid chalky blue and turn richer under bright sun and lean, dry conditions. Trim errant stems and lift and divide clumps every few years to keep the color strong.

planting.note: Root jointed stem segments in spring in cactus mix.

directions: Lay a jointed stem segment on dry cactus mix and wait a few days for the cut to callus before it roots. The plant grows in sausage-like blue-gray sections that drop leaves during summer dormancy, so ease off water then. Give bright light and let it dry completely between drinks to avoid rot.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring in very gritty, dry mix.

directions: Take a cutting, let it callus for several days, then set it in a spare, mineral mix and water very sparingly. The chalk-white, wool-felted leaves are prone to rot, so give strong light, excellent drainage, and reduced water in summer heat. Handle it gently to preserve the silvery felt that gives the plant its name.

planting.note: Root stem or rhizome cuttings in spring in cactus mix.

directions: Detach an upright stem, let it callus, and pot it into fast-draining mix where it roots from spreading underground runners. The angular blue-green stems are marked with purple lines and toothy soft spines, and they need bright light with cautious watering. In good conditions it rewards you with vivid red-orange brush flowers.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring; train as a climber or trailer.

directions: Root stem cuttings in a light succulent mix and provide a trellis or hanging support for the twining stems. The thick, ivy-shaped waxy leaves are often edged in cream, and the plant flowers with pale yellow daisies in bright light. Water when the mix dries and pinch the tips to keep growth full rather than sparse.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring in a hanging pot of gritty mix.

directions: Lay cuttings on cactus mix and they root readily along trailing stems that cascade over a pot rim. The overlapping paddle leaves flush purple in strong sun and cool weather, deepening the display. Give bright light and dry-down between waterings, and enjoy the small orange autumn flowers that carry a musky scent.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring in sharply drained soil.

directions: Take a stem cutting, let it callus, and root it in gritty mix in a bright spot. The blue-green leaves are shaped like arrowheads or spear points and give the plant a striking sculptural look. Water only when dry and provide full sun to keep the leaf color and form crisp.

planting.note: Divide offsets or root leaves in spring in mineral mix.

directions: Separate offsets or root plump leaves in a spare, fast-draining mix and water lightly until established. The finger-like leaves wear a peelable silvery-white felt over green, forming a low rosette-like clump. Give strong light and minimal water, easing off further during its summer rest, and it may lift yellow daisies on tall stalks.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Root stem cuttings in a dry cactus mix and grow them into an upright, branching subshrub. The oval blue-gray leaves turn on edge to reduce midday sun and are rimmed in purple that intensifies in bright light. Water sparingly, prune to shape, and expect small yellow flowers on tall stems in the warm season.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring in lean, fast-draining ground.

directions: Set hardwood-like cuttings into gritty soil where they root and build a dense evergreen shrub. The finger-shaped green leaves clothe upright stems, and in autumn to winter the plant carries showy clusters of sweetly scented golden-yellow flowers. Grow it in full sun with sharp drainage and little supplemental water once established.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Set out a nursery plant in free-draining soil in full sun, where it forms a mounded silver-leaved shrub once known as Senecio 'Sunshine'. The oval leaves are gray-green above with white-felted undersides, and yellow daisy flowers open in early summer. Shear it after bloom, or before if you prefer foliage, to keep it dense and prevent it going leggy.

planting.note: Sow in fall for cold stratification or plant divisions in spring.

directions: Surface-sow seed in autumn so winter cold breaks dormancy, or divide established clumps in spring. This North American native, formerly classed as Senecio aureus, spreads by runners into an evergreen groundcover of rounded basal leaves. It sends up airy stems of bright yellow daisies in mid to late spring and thrives in moist part shade.

planting.note: Sow after frost danger passes for a low mound of ferny foliage and small edible flowers.

directions: Scatter seed lightly on warm soil and press it in, thinning seedlings once they have a few true leaves. Plants form a tidy lacy dome smothered in tiny single flowers that need little deadheading. The citrus-scented, edible blooms begin around eight weeks and carry on all summer.

planting.note: Grown as a perennial in mild zones and a warm-season annual elsewhere; also propagated by division.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and keep it warm, as it can be slow to sprout over two weeks. The anise-scented leaves make a tarragon substitute and can be snipped through the season once the plant is established. Clusters of small golden flowers open in late summer and fall as days shorten.

planting.note: A tall, vigorous annual often planted to help suppress soil nematodes and aggressive weeds.

directions: Sow directly in warm soil and thin to give the towering stems room to develop. The strongly aromatic foliage is used as a herb and the dense roots are grown to discourage nematodes in a rotation. Small cream-and-yellow flower clusters appear late in the season on plants that can reach head height.

planting.note: Grown mainly for its finely divided, fragrant foliage rather than its tiny flowers.

directions: Start seed indoors and transplant after frost into a warm, sunny bed or edging. The threadlike leaves form a soft green cushion prized for texture and its light anise scent. Insignificant creamy flowers may appear late in the season on well-grown plants.

planting.note: An old-fashioned single French type sown once the soil warms in spring.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in warm soil and keep moist until seedlings emerge. This heirloom bears single golden-yellow flowers marked with a bold maroon blotch at the center. Deadhead regularly and blooms will keep coming from early summer to frost.

planting.note: A double French marigold in warm rust and gold tones; sow after frost.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm soil and thin the seedlings to give each plant room. The fully double blooms show russet-red petals edged and reversed in gold. Remove faded flowers to encourage a steady flush through the summer.

planting.note: A compact, early, large-flowered double French series for beds and packs.

directions: Start seed indoors for early transplants or sow directly once soil warms. The uniform plants carry big double crested flowers in yellow, gold, orange, and bicolor shades. Deadhead spent blooms to keep the tidy mounds flowering into fall.

planting.note: A well-branched double French series that flowers early and holds up in heat.

directions: Sow indoors under warmth and set out sturdy transplants after the last frost. Large crested double flowers come in a range of yellows, oranges, and red-flecked bicolors on compact plants. Pinch early and deadhead often for a dense, continuous display.

planting.note: One of the earliest and most compact double French series, good for small pots.

directions: Start seed warm and transplant the dwarf seedlings into full sun. The very short plants pack tightly and cover themselves in early double crested blooms in gold and orange tones. Their small size suits edging and container mixes, flowering right up to frost.

planting.note: A large-flowered double French series with unusually big, flat-faced blooms.

directions: Sow indoors for transplants that establish quickly in warm soil. The oversized double flowers sit above compact foliage in shades of yellow, orange, red, and tangerine. Keep old heads picked off to prolong the bloom into early autumn.

planting.note: A dwarf, large-flowered double French series bred for uniform bedding.

directions: Start the seed warm and move sturdy plugs into the garden after frost. The short, even plants carry big double crested flowers in solid and bicolor shades. Regular deadheading keeps the compact mounds blooming all season.

planting.note: A double French type with strongly patterned red-and-gold bicolor flowers.

directions: Sow into warm soil at a shallow depth and keep the bed moist until germination. The crested double blooms are boldly splashed mahogany-red over a gold base, each one slightly different. Snip faded flowers to keep the plants full and flowering into fall.

planting.note: An old dwarf French mixture of crested double flowers in warm colors.

directions: Direct sow after frost and thin the young plants to stand a hand's width apart. This mix produces frilly crested double blooms in gold, orange, and red bicolors. Deadhead through summer and the bushy plants keep flowering until cold weather.

planting.note: A single-flowered dwarf French series that is very early and heat-tough.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep evenly moist until seedlings appear. The wide single blooms open flat in yellow, orange, and rich red-and-gold patterns that attract pollinators. Little deadheading is needed and flowering runs from early summer to frost.

planting.note: An early double French series with large anemone-form flowers.

directions: Start seed indoors and set out transplants once frost has passed. The full double flowers have a tufted crested center over a collar of flat petals in yellow, orange, and bicolors. Keep spent heads removed to maintain a long, dense flush of color.

planting.note: A large-flowered double French series that stays neat and uniform in beds.

directions: Grow warm from seed and transplant into full sun after the last frost. Plants form even mounds topped with big crested double flowers in solid and striped shades. Deadhead often and the compact plants bloom steadily through the growing season.

planting.note: A tall old African variety grown for huge cutting blooms; give it a long season.

directions: Start seed indoors well before the last frost so plants have time to reach full size. The tall stems carry very large fully double flowers in gold, orange, and yellow. Support taller plants and cut blooms freely for the vase, which encourages more to form until frost.

planting.note: A compact, early African hybrid series with big double flowers on short plants.

directions: Sow indoors under warmth and transplant sturdy seedlings after frost. Unlike older African types these plants stay short and weather-resistant while carrying large double blooms in yellow, gold, and orange. Deadhead to keep the dense plants flowering through summer.

planting.note: A very compact African series with large flowers held on short, uniform plants.

directions: Start warm indoors and move transplants out after the danger of frost. The dwarf plants bear big fully double flowers in primrose, yellow, gold, and orange that sit right above the foliage. Their short habit suits containers and front-of-border planting through the season.

planting.note: A rare creamy-white African marigold prized for its unusual pale blooms.

directions: Start seed indoors and grow on warm, as the white types can be a little slower. Upright plants produce large double flowers in soft creamy white that stand out against the green foliage. Remove faded heads and the plants keep blooming until the first frost.

planting.note: A mid-tall African series with strong stems suited to cutting.

directions: Sow indoors early and set well-hardened plants out after frost. The sturdy, well-branched stems hold large round double flowers in gold, orange, and yellow that are good for arrangements. Cut or deadhead regularly to keep new buds forming.

planting.note: A tall African mixture with very large ball-shaped blooms for the back of the border.

directions: Give this mix a long warm start indoors so the tall stems mature fully. Big dense double flowers in yellow, gold, and orange top the vigorous plants and last well when cut. Stake in exposed sites and keep cutting to prolong flowering into fall.

planting.note: A dwarf African series with large flowers on very short, weather-tolerant plants.

directions: Start seed warm indoors and transplant compact plugs after frost. These plants stay unusually short yet carry big dense double flowers in gold, yellow, and orange that shed rain well. Their low habit makes them a good bedding and container choice all summer.

planting.note: A very dwarf, mounded African series with large flowers held close to the foliage.

directions: Sow warm and set out even, compact transplants after the last frost. The tight cushion of foliage is topped with large double flowers in yellow and orange that need little support. Deadhead lightly to keep the low mounds neat and flowering.

planting.note: A signet type with lacy foliage and countless tiny lemon-yellow edible flowers.

directions: Sow lightly on warm soil and thin to let each plant form its rounded mound. Ferny, citrus-scented leaves are covered with small single yellow flowers that are edible and good for garnish. The tidy plants rarely need deadheading and bloom right through summer.

planting.note: A signet marigold smothered in tiny bright orange edible flowers.

directions: Scatter seed on warm soil, press in, and thin the seedlings once established. The delicate foliage forms a dome studded with small single tangerine-orange flowers that carry a citrus scent. The edible blooms are lovely for edging and container edges and flower all season.

planting.note: A signet type with small rusty-red flowers over fine lacy foliage.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep moist until the fine seedlings appear. Plants form a low ferny mound dotted with small single flowers in mahogany-red edged with gold. The neat mounds are ideal for edging and need almost no deadheading.

planting.note: A dwarf signet with deep red-orange single flowers edged in gold.

directions: Press the fine seed onto warm soil and thin plants to a hand's width apart. Ferny aromatic foliage is covered in small single flowers of brick red rimmed with a thin gold edge. The compact mounds bloom continuously and suit pots, edging, and the herb garden.

planting.note: A signet mixture blending yellow, orange, and red single-flowered plants.

directions: Sow the fine seed on warm soil and thin to give the mounds room to fill in. This mix produces lacy plants smothered in small single flowers spanning gold, orange, and red bicolors. The edible, citrus-scented blooms carry on all summer with little care.

planting.note: A sterile triploid cross of French and African types that blooms nonstop in heat.

directions: Start seed indoors, noting that triploid seed can germinate a bit unevenly. Because the flowers set no seed the plants pour their energy into continuous bloom, even in summer heat. Enjoy the large double flowers in gold, orange, and red from early summer until frost with no deadheading needed.

planting.note: A compact, sterile triploid series that flowers heavily and tolerates heat well.

directions: Sow warm indoors and transplant after frost into full sun. As a seedless triploid it keeps blooming through hot weather without pausing to set seed. The dwarf plants carry large double flowers in yellow, gold, orange, and red and need no deadheading.

planting.note: A French type whose double flowers shift color from red through bronze to gold as they age.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep moist until seedlings emerge. Each double bloom opens deep red and gradually changes through bronze and orange to old gold, so the plant shows several shades at once. Deadhead to keep the color-changing display coming until frost.

planting.note: A French type whose blooms open rosy-pink and shift with cool weather and age.

directions: Start seed warm indoors and transplant after the last frost. The large anemone-form flowers open in shades of rose, pink, and cream that deepen and change as temperatures cool. Cooler nights bring out the richest tones, so expect the best color in spring and fall.

planting.note: Begin indoors in mid-spring and transplant after the danger of frost has passed.

directions: Press seed lightly under a quarter inch of mix and keep it warm until sprouts emerge in two to three weeks. This selection opens in warm terracotta, ivory, and dusky rose shades, so let several plants mingle on one support for the layered effect. Deadhead lightly and blooms continue through the heat of summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring and set out once the soil has warmed.

directions: Cover seed with a scant quarter inch of soil and keep the flat warm to speed germination. This mix blooms in soft apricot, buff, and cinnamon tones, and it climbs well on a low obelisk or spills from a hanging basket. Keep it evenly watered and pick off spent flowers to prolong the display into autumn.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and transplant when frosts are safely past.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in warm mix and expect twining shoots within two to three weeks. This classic form carries bright orange petals around a deep chocolate eye and stays a manageable size for pots and small trellises. Water steadily through summer and it will flower nonstop until cool weather arrives.

planting.note: Begin indoors six weeks early and set out after the last frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it warm and moist until it germinates. Clear golden-yellow flowers with a dark central eye cover the compact vines all season. Provide a trellis or basket edge to climb and deadhead now and then to keep new buds forming.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring, hardening off before transplanting outside.

directions: Set seed a quarter inch under warm mix and keep it lightly damp during germination. Creamy-white flowers with a dark throat give a cooler look that pairs well with brighter forms of the vine. Train the stems onto a support early and keep watering consistent for bloom right up to frost.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and plant out once the weather settles warm.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in a warm spot and thin to the strongest seedlings. The two-toned red and orange petals give a fiery look that stands out on a basket or trellis. Keep the roots moist and blooms keep coming from early summer through the first cool nights.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring and transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Barely bury the seed in warm mix and keep it evenly moist while it sprouts. This unusual selection opens in muted bronze and buff tones with a dark eye for a smoky, antique look. Give it something to climb and steady water, and it flowers freely until frost.

planting.note: Begin indoors and move out when nights are reliably mild.

directions: Plant seed a quarter inch deep and keep the flat in a warm, bright place until it germinates. Soft rose-pink flowers with darker centers give a gentler tone than the usual oranges and yellows. Train onto a trellis or let it trail from a basket and deadhead to keep the buds coming.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring and set plants out after the last frost.

directions: Cover the seed with a scant quarter inch of mix and keep it warm to germinate in two to three weeks. Deep brick-red petals surround a black eye for one of the richest colors in the group. Provide a small support, water regularly, and enjoy blooms from midsummer to frost.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant once the soil warms in late spring.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm mix and keep it lightly moist until seedlings appear. Warm apricot-orange flowers with a dark center glow against the fresh green foliage. Give it a trellis or basket to fill and remove old blooms to encourage a steady flush all summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring and plant out after frost has passed.

directions: Set seed a quarter inch deep in warm mix and keep it evenly damp during germination. This vigorous mix blends soft apricot, rose, ivory, and pale red flowers, often with only a faint eye. Let it climb a tall trellis or cascade from a large basket, and keep it watered for bloom until frost.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and transplant when nights stay above the mid-fifties.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in warm mix and keep it moist until the shoots twine upward. Bright pumpkin-orange flowers with a dark eye cover this free-blooming selection. Give it a support or basket edge and consistent water, and pinch spent flowers to keep new ones forming.

planting.note: Begin indoors and set out once the danger of frost is over.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep the flat warm and bright during germination. Cheerful lemon-yellow petals with a near-black eye give strong contrast on a trellis or in a basket. Water evenly and remove faded flowers, and it will bloom from summer into fall.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring and transplant after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant seed a quarter inch deep in warm mix and keep it lightly moist until it sprouts. Each flower is boldly striped in red and orange with a dark eye for a striking pinwheel effect. Train onto a support or let it trail, water regularly, and deadhead to keep the color coming until frost.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out when the soil is warm.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in warm mix and expect germination in two to three weeks. This selection is prized for extra-large orange flowers with a dark central eye. Give it a sturdy trellis, keep it evenly watered, and it blooms generously through the warm months.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring and transplant once frost has passed.

directions: Cover seed with a scant quarter inch of soil and keep it warm while it germinates. Warm salmon and soft coral tones give this vine a gentle, sunset feel on a trellis or basket. Water consistently and remove old blooms, and flowering continues from summer to frost.

planting.note: Begin indoors and set out after the last frost of spring.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in warm mix and keep it evenly moist for germination. Pure white petals surrounding a dark eye give a crisp, cooling effect among greener plantings. Provide a support to climb, water steadily, and blooms carry on until cool nights end the season.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring and transplant once nights turn mild.

directions: Barely cover the seed in warm mix and keep it lightly damp until it sprouts. Muted brick and clay tones with a dark eye lend this vine an earthy, understated charm. Train it up a trellis or let it spill from a pot, and keep it watered for bloom into autumn.

subcategory: tender perennial vine grown as annual

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant only after the soil is thoroughly warm.

directions: Plant seed about a quarter inch deep in warm mix and keep it moist until it germinates. This twining vine bears pure white five-lobed flowers that are lightly scented and lack a dark eye. Give it a trellis or fence, water in dry spells, and enjoy blooms through the warm season; note it can self-sow freely in mild climates.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring after frost, as it is usually grown from vegetative starts.

directions: Grow this vine from rooted cuttings and give it a heavy-duty arbor or wall to cover. It carries the same big trumpet flowers as the species but in soft white with a creamy throat. Provide steady moisture in the heat, feed during growth, and prune after each bloom flush to control its considerable spread.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings in spring once frost is past.

directions: Root cuttings in warm, moist conditions and plant against a strong support in frost-free ground. This vigorous vine has glossy laurel-like leaves and hanging pale blue trumpet flowers. Keep it well watered, feed during growth, and prune hard after flowering, as it can become invasive where winters are warm.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring in a warm, sheltered spot free of frost.

directions: Grow from rooted cuttings and train onto a tall, sturdy pergola where the flower chains can hang free. This spectacular vine drops long pendent racemes of hooded red-brown and yellow flowers that lure hummingbirds and sunbirds. Give it rich, moist soil, some shade from the harshest sun, and shelter from wind so the flower chains are not damaged.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings in spring in a frost-free, sheltered position.

directions: Propagate from cuttings and give this vine a tall support where its long flower chains can hang. It bears pendent racemes of small scarlet flowers over broad tropical foliage. Keep the soil evenly moist, protect it from frost and drying wind, and prune after flowering to shape and refresh the plant.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings in spring once frost danger has passed.

directions: Grow this shrubby thunbergia from rooted cuttings and space plants for a soft, rounded form. It bears pure white trumpet flowers with a yellow throat against dark green leaves. Water steadily, prune lightly after bloom flushes to keep it compact, and it flowers repeatedly in warm weather.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost; usually started from cuttings or division.

directions: Set out rooted cuttings or divisions in spring in warm, well-drained soil. This semi-scrambling perennial produces deep royal-blue trumpet flowers with golden-yellow throats. Give it a low support or let it sprawl through a border, water in dry spells, and cut it back after flowering to keep it tidy.

planting.note: Sow indoors under warmth and transplant after frost danger passes.

directions: Surface-sow and leave the seed uncovered so light can reach it, keeping the tray warm and moist. This compact, well-branched selection needs no pinching and holds a tidy mound in beds or pots. Enjoy the deep blue-and-white flowers from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Begin seed indoors and set out when the soil has warmed.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of a warm, moist tray and press lightly without burying it. Grow the compact plants in dappled light and keep the roots from drying to avoid stalled bloom. The saturated purple-blue flowers carry a yellow throat and appear all season.

planting.note: Start early indoors, then transplant into warm ground or containers.

directions: Leave the fine seed on the surface for light and mist to keep it damp until sprouts show. Place the neat mounds where they escape harsh afternoon sun and feed lightly through summer. Rosy-pink blooms with a golden throat cover the plant continuously.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and plant out after the last frost.

directions: Press seed onto moist mix without covering and hold steady warmth for even sprouting. The compact habit lights up shady borders and shaded patio pots without staking. Clean white flowers with a soft yellow eye bloom from summer to first frost.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and shift outdoors once nights are reliably warm.

directions: Sow on the surface for light and keep the tray warm and evenly moist until germination. This tidy, low-growing selection thrives in part shade and steady moisture. Its soft yellow flowers brighten shaded plantings right through the season.

planting.note: Start under lights indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the small seed and mist to settle it, keeping warmth constant for a quick, even stand. Grow in filtered light with consistent watering to keep the mounds full. Vivid magenta blooms with a bright throat flower nonstop into autumn.

planting.note: Raise the blend indoors and plant out once weather settles.

directions: Leave the seed uncovered on damp mix and keep it warm until seedlings appear. This uniform blend combines blue, rose, white and yellow shades on matched, compact plants. Expect a colorful mix of throated blooms from summer until frost.

planting.note: Sow indoors ahead of the season and transplant when frost has passed.

directions: Press seed onto the surface for light and hold the tray warm and moist for good germination. This early, dwarf selection stays compact in beds and mixed containers. Two-toned blue flowers with pale and dark markings bloom heavily all summer.

planting.note: Begin indoors and set out after the danger of frost.

directions: Sow on the surface, keep uncovered for light, and maintain even warmth until sprouts emerge. The dwarf, well-branched plants suit edging and shaded pots without pinching. Rose-pink blooms with a paler face open steadily from early summer on.

planting.note: Start seed warm indoors and transplant after frost.

directions: Leave the fine seed on top of moist mix so light reaches it and keep it warm. Grow the compact mounds in filtered light with steady moisture for full plants. Deep burgundy-violet flowers with a yellow throat bloom through the warm months.

planting.note: Raise indoors early and move outside once nights warm.

directions: Surface-sow and press gently, keeping the tray warm and damp until germination. This dwarf, early-flowering plant fills shaded borders and window boxes neatly. Rich violet blooms with contrasting throat markings appear all season.

planting.note: Sow the blend indoors and transplant after frost danger.

directions: Keep the seed uncovered on moist medium and warm until an even stand appears. This compact blend mixes blue, rose, violet and burgundy shades on matched plants. A colorful patchwork of throated flowers carries from summer to frost.

planting.note: Start indoors under warmth and plant out after frost.

directions: Sow seed on the surface for light and keep the medium moist and warm until sprouting. The very compact, uniform plants are ideal for pack production and small pots. Large blue-and-white flowers cover the low mounds through summer.

planting.note: Begin indoors early and transplant when weather is warm.

directions: Leave the fine seed uncovered and mist to keep it damp under steady warmth. This dwarf, early series blooms young and stays tidy in shade plantings. Soft sky-blue flowers with pale throats open freely all season.

planting.note: Sow the blend indoors and set out after frost.

directions: Press seed onto moist mix without covering and keep warm until an even flush emerges. The compact, matched plants blend several colors for pack and container sales. A mix of large throated blooms fills the mounds through the warm season.

planting.note: Start indoors early for the fast, dwarf plants and transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow for light and hold even warmth and moisture for quick germination. This very compact, early series flowers young in small pots and packs. Bright pink blooms with a yellow throat cover the tidy plants all summer.

planting.note: Sow warm indoors and plant out once frost has passed.

directions: Leave seed on the surface and keep it moist under steady warmth for a fast, even stand. The dwarf plants suit small containers and shaded edging without pinching. Deep blue flowers with lighter faces and a yellow eye bloom nonstop.

planting.note: Set out started plants once the soil is warm and frost has passed.

directions: Plant vegetative starts at the same depth they grew in the pot in rich, moist soil. This vigorous, spreading type trails over basket edges and needs no deadheading. Large blue flowers with a yellow throat pour out from planting until frost.

common_name: Summer Wave Large Violet Trailing Torenia

planting.note: Transplant after frost into warm, well-watered soil or baskets.

directions: Set plants into fertile, moisture-retentive soil and water in well to establish. The trailing, heat-tolerant habit spills from containers and covers ground quickly. Oversized violet blooms with a golden throat carry through the whole season.

planting.note: Plant started stock after the last frost in warm soil.

directions: Place vegetative starts in rich, evenly moist soil and give them part shade in hot regions. The mounding, trailing plant fills baskets and mixed pots without staking. Amethyst-purple flowers with a bright center bloom continuously into fall.

common_name: Summer Wave Bouquet Deep Blue Torenia

planting.note: Set out after frost; the mounded Bouquet type suits smaller pots.

directions: Plant the started stock at pot depth in moist, fertile soil and water in. This more mounded, compact member of the series fills quarts and small baskets fast. Deep blue flowers with a yellow throat bloom heavily from spring planting to frost.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into warm, moist soil or containers.

directions: Set vegetative plants into rich soil and keep the roots consistently moist. The compact, mounding habit works in mixed pots and shady borders alike. Warm yellow-gold flowers keep coming from planting through early autumn.

planting.note: Plant started stock once frost has passed and soil is warm.

directions: Set the vegetative plant at pot depth in fertile, moisture-holding soil and water well. This mounding-to-trailing selection loves warmth and humidity with part shade. Rich blue flowers with a yellow throat bloom from planting straight to frost.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into warm, well-drained but moist soil.

directions: Plant started stock in rich soil and keep it evenly moist in a part-shade spot. The trailing habit brightens shaded baskets and combination pots without deadheading. Soft white flowers with a pale throat glow through the warm months.

planting.note: Set out after frost in warm, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant the vegetative start at pot depth and water in to settle the roots. This vigorous trailing type thrives in heat and humidity given part shade. Purple flowers marked with a golden-yellow throat bloom from planting to frost.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost into warm, moist soil.

directions: Set started plants into fertile soil and keep moisture steady in part shade. The mounding, spreading habit fills baskets and shaded beds quickly. Deep midnight-blue flowers with a yellow eye carry through the season.

planting.note: Plant started stock after frost in warm, moisture-holding soil.

directions: Set the plant at pot depth in rich soil and water it in thoroughly. This trailing selection wants warmth, humidity and afternoon shade to look its best. Clear pink flowers with a yellow throat bloom nonstop until frost.

planting.note: Set out plants or rooted cuttings once the soil is warm.

directions: Root soft cuttings or set nursery plants into moist, humus-rich soil. This low, spreading species trails as a groundcover or over pot edges and roots at the nodes. Solid blue-violet flowers appear over a long warm-season stretch.

planting.note: Sow the large seeds outdoors once the soil has warmed and frost has passed.

directions: Nick or soak the pea-sized seeds overnight and sow them about half an inch deep where they are to grow, since seedlings resent transplanting. Keep the soil lean and avoid heavy feeding, as rich ground produces lush foliage but few blooms. Flowers appear roughly two months after sowing and continue until frost; pick young leaves and blossoms for a peppery salad garnish.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in autumn and expect slow, erratic germination over winter.

directions: Set the roots in cool, moist, humus-rich soil with the crown shaded but the stems free to climb into sun through a shrub or hedge. This Chilean perennial resents dry heat and thrives where summers stay cool and damp. Vivid scarlet flowers open in mid to late summer, followed by small blue fruits.

planting.note: Sow in autumn into gritty soil; the deep rootstock dislikes disturbance once settled.

directions: Give this Andean perennial sharp drainage and a sunny spot where its long taproot can run deep. Silvery-blue trailing stems fan out over the ground and carry masses of yellow flowers in early summer. It dies back after flowering, so mark its place to avoid digging into the dormant crown.

planting.note: Pot tubers in autumn for winter growth under frost-free glass in cold regions.

directions: Start the small tubers in a gritty, free-draining mix and keep them barely moist until growth begins. Thread-like stems climb through a support and carry curious tubular flowers of red, yellow, and blue-black in late winter and spring. Withhold water once the foliage yellows and let the tuber rest dry through summer.

planting.note: Sow directly after frost; this heirloom stays compact and bushy.

directions: Sow the seeds half an inch deep in lean soil and full sun for the best flowering. This classic bush variety pairs deep blue-green foliage with velvety crimson-scarlet blooms held above the leaves. Deadhead lightly and enjoy edible flowers from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost for its cream-marbled variegated leaves.

directions: Plant seeds half an inch deep where the striking white-splashed foliage can be admired up close. The bushy mounds carry flowers in a mix of red, orange, yellow, and cream that sit brightly against the marbled leaves. Keep soil on the lean side and pick blooms regularly to prolong the display.

planting.note: Sow after frost for a compact mound of semi-double flowers.

directions: Set seeds half an inch deep and thin to a hand's width apart in a sunny, well-drained bed. This dwarf strain bears fully double and semi-double blooms in a range of warm colors held clear of the foliage. Deadhead to keep the flush coming through the whole season.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost for its soft cream-and-red flowers.

directions: Sow the seeds half an inch deep in full sun and lean soil for the richest bloom. This tidy bush type shows pale creamy-yellow flowers each marked with a bright orange-red splash at the throat. Blooms sit above the leaves and last from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Sow after frost; upward-facing, nearly spurless flowers top the foliage.

directions: Plant seeds half an inch deep and thin to about a foot apart in a sunny spot. This dwarf series holds its semi-double, spurless flowers facing upward and out over the mounded leaves for an open display. Pick spent blooms to keep the plants flowering into autumn.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; a very old, compact dwarf mix.

directions: Sow the seeds half an inch deep and space the little plants a hand's width apart. This heirloom dwarf mix forms neat mounds ideal for edging, pots, and window boxes with single flowers in classic warm shades. Blooms appear about seven weeks from sowing and repeat if you keep them picked.

planting.note: Sow after frost; semi-trailing habit good for baskets and banks.

directions: Plant seeds half an inch deep and give the semi-trailing stems room to spill or ramble. Fragrant, semi-double golden-yellow flowers stand up well above the foliage on this classic Gleam-series plant. Let it trail from a container or cover a slope, and deadhead to keep the show going.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost for a trailing sheet of orange-scarlet.

directions: Sow seeds half an inch deep and let this semi-trailing Gleam type sprawl over walls or tumble from baskets. Large semi-double flowers in bright orange-scarlet are lightly fragrant and hold clear of the round leaves. Keep the soil poor and sunny for the heaviest bloom.

planting.note: Sow after frost; rich mahogany-red semi-double flowers on trailing stems.

directions: Push the seeds half an inch into lean, sunny soil and allow the trailing stems to spread. Semi-double blooms of deep mahogany-red carry a light scent and stand above the foliage. Grow it in a basket or as ground cover and remove faded flowers to extend the season.

planting.note: Sow at a trellis after frost; long-trailing climber in pale yellow.

directions: Plant seeds half an inch deep beside netting or a trellis and guide the long stems as they climb. Soft, pale primrose-yellow flowers glow against the foliage and cover the vine through summer. Provide support early, since this vigorous trailer can reach several feet by season's end.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost for its near-black, deep maroon flowers.

directions: Sow the seeds half an inch deep in a sunny bed with lean soil. This compact bush variety carries single flowers of such deep maroon-red that they read as black against bright green leaves. Blooms sit above the foliage and last from summer to frost.

planting.note: Sow after frost for soft creamy-white flowers on a bushy plant.

directions: Set seeds half an inch deep and thin the mounded plants to about a foot apart. Pale, creamy buttermilk flowers cover this compact bush and blend easily with any color scheme. Keep the soil lean and pick the edible blooms often to keep more coming.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost for bright cherry-rose semi-double flowers.

directions: Sow seeds half an inch deep in full sun and thin to a hand's width apart. This Jewel-type dwarf produces semi-double flowers in a vivid cherry-rose pink held above the round leaves. Deadhead through the season and grow it in beds or containers.

planting.note: Sow after frost for soft salmon-pink blooms on a compact plant.

directions: Plant the seeds half an inch deep in lean, sunny ground and thin to about a foot apart. This tidy bush variety bears semi-double flowers in a gentle salmon-pink that is unusual among nasturtiums. Keep it deadheaded and it will flower steadily until frost.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; an old variety with salmon flowers and dark leaves.

directions: Sow seeds half an inch deep in a warm, sunny bed with poor soil. This heirloom pairs coppery, purple-tinged foliage with glowing salmon-rose flowers for a warm contrast. Blooms rise above the leaves and continue from early summer until the first frost.

planting.note: Sow after frost for its unusual split, flame-shaped petals.

directions: Sow the seeds half an inch deep and thin to about a foot apart in full sun. This novel bush type has deeply split, flame-like petals in a mix of red, orange, and yellow that give a spiky, fringed look. Keep the soil lean and deadhead to keep the quirky flowers coming.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost for creamy flowers blotched with red.

directions: Plant seeds half an inch deep in a sunny, well-drained spot with lean soil. This compact variety shows pale cream flowers each splashed with a raspberry-red throat, giving a two-tone effect. Grow it in pots or borders and pick blooms often to prolong flowering.

planting.note: Sow after frost for rose-pink flowers marked at the throat.

directions: Sow seeds half an inch deep and thin the bushy plants to a hand's width apart. Single flowers in a warm rose-pink, each with a darker spotted throat, stand well above the round leaves. Keep the soil poor and deadhead to keep the compact plants blooming into autumn.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; flowers face up and out above the leaves.

directions: Push seeds half an inch into lean, sunny soil and thin to about a foot apart. This Tip Top selection holds deep mahogany-red flowers upward and clear of the foliage for an unobstructed view. Deadhead regularly and enjoy blooms from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Sow after frost for cream flowers splashed and speckled with red.

directions: Sow the seeds half an inch deep in a sunny bed with lean soil. Each creamy flower is uniquely splashed and streaked with red, so no two blooms look quite alike. Grow this compact bush in beds or pots and keep it deadheaded for continuous color.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost for a striking dark-and-light contrast.

directions: Plant seeds half an inch deep and space the bushy plants about a foot apart. This mix combines near-black deep-red flowers with pale creamy-white ones for a bold light-and-dark planting. Keep the soil lean and sunny and pick spent blooms to keep it flowering.

planting.note: Sow after frost for orange flowers over cream-variegated leaves.

directions: Sow seeds half an inch deep in full sun and thin to about a foot apart. This bushy variety sets bright orange flowers against boldly cream-marbled foliage for a lively two-tone effect. Keep the ground lean and deadhead to maintain the display into autumn.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; scarlet flowers over dark blue-green leaves.

directions: Sow the seeds half an inch deep in a warm, sunny spot with lean soil. This old favorite sets deep scarlet-red flowers against dark, blue-tinged foliage for strong contrast. Blooms carry above the leaves and continue steadily from summer to frost when kept picked.

planting.note: Start seed indoors well before the last frost, then set plants out once nights stay warm.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it in darkness at a steady warm temperature, as germination is slow and uneven. Pinch young plants once to build a bushy mound and water at the base to keep foliage dry. Expect dense clusters of bloom from early summer until frost when grown in full sun.

planting.note: Sow indoors under warmth and transplant after the danger of frost has passed.

directions: Press seed lightly onto warm, moist mix and cover with black plastic to exclude light until sprouts appear. Grow on in bright light and harden off before planting into beds or containers. This compact seed strain forms tidy mounds topped with broad flower heads all season.

planting.note: Begin seed indoors and move seedlings outdoors once warm settled weather arrives.

directions: Sow into a warm, sterile medium and keep evenly moist while covered against light. Set out well-branched plants after frost and give them the sunniest spot available. This vigorous seed series is prized for uniform, weather-tolerant color in mass plantings.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors from an early sowing and plant out after the last frost.

directions: Start seed early indoors, keeping trays dark and warm until germination completes. Transplant hardened seedlings into full sun and pinch once to encourage spreading growth. This award-winning strain opens apricot flowers that soften to creamy tones as they age.

planting.note: Sow indoors under warmth and set out well after the last spring frost.

directions: Cover the fine seed lightly and hold trays dark and warm to trigger sprouting. Grow seedlings in strong light and harden them before planting into beds or edging. This early, compact strain flowers close to the ground in a broad range of clear colors.

planting.note: Begin seed indoors and transplant into a warm, sunny bed after frost.

directions: Cover seed thinly and keep warm and moist until sprouts emerge. Plant in poor to average soil that drains freely, since rich or wet ground weakens the stems. This selection carries deep purple flower heads on upright stalks that thrive through summer heat.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after the last frost once the soil has warmed.

directions: Set out nursery plants at the same depth they grew in the pot and space for spreading mats. Water to establish, then let this tough selection dry between soakings in full sun. Rich magenta-purple flowers cover the trailing stems from spring through fall, especially after a light shearing.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost once soil warms.

directions: Set transplants into gritty, well-drained soil and water only to establish. Give full sun and shear lightly midseason to keep the mat dense and blooming. This selection is loved for bright rose-pink flowers on ferny, spreading foliage.

planting.note: Start seed indoors early and transplant into sun after frost.

directions: Keep the sown tray dark and warm until sprouts appear, then grow on in strong light. Set out into full sun and let the deeply cut, trailing stems tumble over edges and baskets. This vigorous seed strain blooms violet-blue almost nonstop and tolerates heat and drought well.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Set transplants shallowly and water in, then keep on the dry side once rooted. Give full sun and sharp drainage where the low stems creep and root along the ground. Brilliant scarlet-red flower clusters cover the plant from late spring through fall.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost once nights stay mild.

directions: Set nursery plants into rich, freely draining soil or containers and water to establish. Feed lightly through summer and shear if growth thins in midseason to renew bloom. This mildew-resistant vegetative series carries large flower heads on trailing stems all season.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after the last frost.

directions: Plant into well-drained beds or pots and keep evenly moist while roots take hold. This more compact, heat-loving series needs little deadheading to stay tidy. Vivid flower clusters cover the mounding, semi-trailing stems from spring through frost.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost into a sunny, well-drained site.

directions: Set transplants into loose, well-drained soil and water gently until established. This fine-textured, mildew-resistant series forms low, spreading mats ideal for baskets and ground cover. Feathery foliage carries dense flower heads continuously through the warm season.

planting.note: Plant out rooted cuttings after frost once soil warms.

directions: Plant into rich, well-drained soil and keep moist through establishment. This vigorous series produces oversized, ball-shaped flower heads on strongly trailing stems. Give full sun and an occasional light feeding for heavy bloom all summer.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out after frost into full sun.

directions: Plant into well-drained beds or containers and water to settle the roots. This broad color series branches freely and needs little pinching to stay full. Flowers open steadily on semi-trailing stems from spring until the first hard frost.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost into a warm, sunny bed.

directions: Set plants into freely draining soil and keep lightly moist until established. This large-flowered, mounding series holds up well to heat and resists mildew. Deadheading is rarely needed for continuous color from late spring to frost.

planting.note: Plant out rooted cuttings after the last frost.

directions: Plant into well-drained soil or mixed containers and water to establish. This series is noted for eye-catching bicolors and patterned flower heads. Grow in full sun and shear lightly if midsummer bloom slows.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; hardy where winters are mild.

directions: Set plants into well-drained soil and water through the first season to build roots. This unusually cold- and heat-tolerant series returns as a perennial in mild zones. Its spreading stems bloom heavily through summer with little care beyond a midseason trim.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after frost into full sun.

directions: Plant into well-drained containers or beds and water to settle in. This early, free-branching series trails strongly and fills baskets quickly. Full sun and light feeding keep the flower heads coming through the whole season.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out after frost into a sunny spot.

directions: Plant into well-drained soil and keep evenly moist while establishing. This mounding, semi-trailing series offers a wide palette and good garden vigor. Grow in full sun for dense, season-long clusters of bloom.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Set plants into freely draining soil or baskets and water to establish. This vigorous series trails well and is bred for strong branching and large flower heads. Give full sun and a light midseason feed for continuous summer color.
